Q: Put the majority of final prep energy into Quant because a higher Quant ceiling would stabilize the overall score.
Watched TTP core videos repeatedly to rebuild conceptual clarity from the ground up.
Stayed in the Quant section for the full time even after finishing early, using review to change two answers that still felt uncertain.
Focused on protecting easy and medium questions as the score foundation.
V: Used GMAT Ninja and TTP to build cleaner tools after Magoosh provided only a baseline.
Started with Verbal on the final exam and responded to a testing-center glitch by moving quickly rather than leaving blanks.
Respected the Focus Edition after an earlier 45-day attempt based on assuming no Sentence Correction would make it easier failed badly.
DI: Felt comfortable with DS, Table Analysis, and TPA but shakier on MSR.
Used the optional break before Quant and DI to drink water, breathe, and reset after a disrupted Verbal section.
Was surprised by a high DI result despite uncertainty, reinforcing that felt difficulty is not always diagnostic.
overall: Persisted across GRE and GMAT attempts over more than three years, eventually moving from a 57th-percentile result to 98th percentile.
Used Magoosh for a base, GMAT Ninja for cleaner methods, and TTP for deeper conceptual clarity.
Studied intensely while working full time, often late at night in the final months.
Identified not using available mocks as a mistake and recommended using the mocks already paid for.
Framed perseverance as quiet execution: staying through review time, breathing through disruptions, and not replacing understanding with hacks.

Q: Recognized that GMAT Quant required process skills and application, not just mathematical knowledge.
Used Scholaranium practice to find subtle gaps and understand how the same concept could appear in varied forms.
Focused on relationships between topics, such as rate questions that also required ratios or percentages.
Used timed practice to learn when alternative methods such as testing numbers or working backward were more efficient.
Analyzed misses to distinguish calculation errors from misread constraints or rushed setup.
V: Used conscious reading and pause points after key sentences to avoid mindless reading.
Stopped jumping straight to CR answer choices and practiced pre-thinking until it became natural and time-saving.
For RC, focused on author perspective and argument structure instead of technical details.
Used medium and hard cementing practice to internalize methods and reveal when techniques were not being applied consistently.
DI: Treated DI as a mix of Verbal and Quant skills rather than an isolated section.
Used preliminary scanning of MSR sources before answering questions to understand information connections.
Learned to identify relevant data points and table or graph relationships quickly instead of being overwhelmed by information.
Used DI practice to reinforce both logical reasoning and calculation speed.
overall: Improved from 565 to 685 over six months while working long bank hours.
Used a structured plan to make 30-60 daily study minutes productive.
Valued consistency over long sessions and followed a realistic schedule.
Managed test-day anxiety with breathing exercises and confidence from practice tests.
Focused on understanding and cementing skills rather than merely increasing practice volume.

Q: Improved Quant between attempts by focusing almost entirely on official material and Aditya Kumar's conceptual videos.
Diagnosed the first-attempt Quant drop as a timing issue caused by spending too much time on early questions.
Used official guide practice to sharpen execution and reduced Quant misses sharply on the second attempt.
V: Recommended sticking to official guide books for Verbal and using LSAT questions from GMAT Club as supplementary practice.
Used GMAT Ninja and other free expert content as part of self-prep.
DI: Focused DI prep on the official guide and DI Review.
Viewed DI mainly as a time-management section because TPA and MSR can consume too much time.
Recommended identifying likely time-sink DI questions within the first 15-20 seconds and skipping them to protect time for other questions.
Accepted that not every DI question can be fully attempted under time pressure.
overall: Moved from 645 to 675 on the second official attempt after two years of intermittent self-prep.
Used only official mocks as paid resources and relied heavily on free expert videos and GMAT Club.
Found official mocks closest to the real exam.
Did not recommend on-and-off preparation while balancing work because it made the path less efficient.

Q: Raised Quant from Q83 to Q88 in 15 days by switching from random mixed practice to targeted question-type practice.
Identified Number Properties, Algebra, and some P&C questions as weak points.
Used condensed sectional mocks and daily single-question-type quizzes to build automatic process recognition.
Learned to identify likely five-minute time sinks quickly and skip them with discipline.
Used a three-step Quant habit: read slowly, solve quickly through practiced processes, then reread before marking.
V: Maintained solid Verbal performance through structured process work, despite initially expecting Verbal to be the weakest area.
Started test day with Verbal and treated it like another sectional quiz.
DI: Practiced every DI session under a strict 45-minute constraint because casual DI practice did not build the needed speed.
Targeted weak DI types such as MSR with timed quizzes.
For Data Sufficiency, created only the initial equations needed to judge sufficiency rather than fully solving when unnecessary.
Used short focused DI sessions to stay consistent after work when full MSR-heavy practice felt draining.
Learned that taking Verbal and DI back to back without a break caused mental fatigue late in DI.
overall: Treated mocks as validation checkpoints rather than primary learning tools.
Used targeted quizzes to build skills, then returned to mocks after reaching a plateau.
Kept an error log that asked why each miss happened, not just what was wrong.
Prepared efficiently while working full time by using shorter focused sessions and structured practice.
Built confidence through consistency rather than relying on good test-day luck.

Q: Pushed an already strong Quant score higher by improving hard-question accuracy in Number Properties and Word Problems.
Followed processes meticulously and reviewed error patterns deeply.
Practiced challenging Quant questions regularly through Scholaranium.
Prioritized accuracy first, then gradually improved timing.
V: Improved Verbal through refinement rather than a full rebuild.
Used Master Comprehension to strengthen fundamentals.
Built a structured process for each Verbal question type.
Used pre-thinking as a major CR improvement lever.
Studied why correct answers were correct and why wrong answers were wrong.
DI: Improved DI by recognizing how Verbal and Quant skills intersect in the section.
Built GI through a systematic approach to graph analysis.
Raised DS accuracy through heavier practice with varied DI questions.
Applied RC reading strategies to MSR after studying MSR-specific methods.
Used time-management techniques for complex DI questions.
overall: Started from a relatively strong baseline but used targeted, data-driven improvement to reach 715.
Tracked accuracy improvements by question type instead of judging progress only by total score.
Used regular targeted quizzes, official resources, and thorough post-practice error analysis.
Emphasized process skills and mistake prevention over raw content accumulation.

Q: Recognized that Quant mistakes were often careless or logic errors under time pressure, not missing concepts.
Stopped trying to solve every Quant question perfectly and used plugging numbers or estimation when appropriate.
Tracked careless and logic mistakes separately from concept gaps.
Built awareness that each question had limited time and the approach needed to reflect that constraint.
overall: Broke a 645 plateau and reached 695 in about 16 days while working full time.
Changed review quality rather than simply adding more resources or hours.
Classified mistakes as concept, logic, careless, or timing instead of only checking answers.
Wrote one-line rules for repeated patterns.
Stopped resource hopping and stayed with official material plus GMAT Club.
